text:
hmm I haven't dug into it yet myself, but I ran across this idea called "differential dataflow" that seems to be using the right words. I think one of the contributors to this implementatiion was experimenting with extending it to a React-js like system for transparently generating DOM:

<https://github.com/TimelyDataflow/differential-dataflow>

hmm I haven't dug into it yet myself, but I ran across this idea called "differential dataflow" that seems to be using the right words. I think one of the contributors to this implementatiion was experimenting with extending it to a React-js like system for transparently generating DOM:

<https://github.com/TimelyDataflow/differential-dataflow>

Differential dataflow is an iterative, incremental, stream processor basically. The rust version of Eve was based on taking the main idea behind differential dataflow and specializing it for a datalog-ish semantics. It worked very well, though implementing it is extremely unintuitive.

It's really great work. Frank McSherry (the guy who started it) is one of my absolute favorite people in the space. He's wonderfully irreverent :slightly_smiling_face:

Based on feedback from a few people and the perspective of a new day I decided to reupload with some commentary and a visual indicator of what I'm talking about. If you found the last video difficult to follow, please give this one a try. <https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=4T11-B7jXIk>

root.text:
I've been pretty quiet lately because I've had my head down working on WhiteBox, a tool to show you how your (compiled) code behaves as you write it (similar to Glen Chiacchieri's REPLugger, if you saw that).
I'd like some feedback on what people would like to see more of. I'm thinking about doing a livestream presentation this weekend... I might do some UI mockups to give you an idea of what I'm aiming for (and to get some criticism)
Apologies for the silence, I wanted to get something up quickly... hopefully it makes sense.
EDIT: video replaced with a version with commentary below

attachments.title: WhiteBox (REPL/live debugger) demonstration 1 - live reloading and variable values

I've been pretty quiet lately because I've had my head down working on WhiteBox, a tool to show you how your (compiled) code behaves as you write it (similar to Glen Chiacchieri's REPLugger, if you saw that).
I'd like some feedback on what people would like to see more of. I'm thinking about doing a livestream presentation this weekend... I might do some UI mockups to give you an idea of what I'm aiming for (and to get some criticism)
Apologies for the silence, I wanted to get something up quickly... hopefully it makes sense.

by “scannable” i meant visually—you can display recursion and variable values a bunch of different ways to explain to a user what’s going on in their program. if this is for debugging, you might want to focus on a layout of the pretty print that most emphasizes what’s changing the variables (for example). idk if there’s a doc or vid somewhere elaborating the vision/goals for whitebox but it would depend on those

wow, high praise, thank you very much! :grin:
wow, high praise, thank you very much! :grin:

gotcha, I wasn't sure if you meant searchable via a search box or something...

Something I might not have made clear enough in the video is that I have no real intention of using the command line as a primary UI output. It's currently just the easiest way of giving an indication of what information the program is collecting.

I don't know if there's a good way for sure to make a static (non-animated, non-interactive) display of recursive functions scannable, but my first response is that there are many isomorphic ways of showing hierarchies. I had been playing around with the possibilities myself, but someone put up their interpretation on this slack a short while back (if you caught that).

For most hierarchies I imagine that some of these representations are better than others, and deserve to be experimented with. The best solution may be different for e.g. functions that return a number vs a string vs a struct (numbers are probably the easy case)
